Once the coheight bridge lands as `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, the
`hreg_dim` conjunction in `CodimOneExtension.lean` halves: the
Krull-dim half closes via the bridge; the regular-local-ring half remains

The bridge also lets `Scheme.RationalMap.order` (`WeilDivisor.lean`:148-151)

## Required content

The chapter should contain three lemma blocks + one instance block, each
backed by a `\lean{...}` pin matching the declaration name in the eventual
`Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`. Each block needs a `% SOURCE:` /
`% SOURCE QUOTE:` from the project's analogist recipe + the relevant
Stacks/Mathlib reference, plus a clear informal proof sketch.

### Block 1 — Topology lemma: coheight commutes with open embedding

```latex
\begin{lemma}[coheight on opens]
  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
  Let $X$ be a topological space and $U \subseteq X$ an open subset.
  For any $z \in U$, the coheight (in the specialization preorder) of $z$
  in $U$ equals the coheight of $z$ in $X$.
\end{lemma}
```

Sketch: every strict-generization chain `z = a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ` in `X`
starting at `z` has every `aᵢ ∈ U` because each `aᵢ ⤳ z` and `U` is
open containing `z`. The bijection between chains in `U` and chains in
`X` starting at `z` preserves length.

Source pointer: `analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md` Decision 2 sketch L246-257.

### Block 2 — Algebra-geometry duality: coheight on Spec = height of prime

```latex
\begin{lemma}[coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum]
  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
  Let $R$ be a commutative ring. For any $p \in \Spec R$ (viewed as a
  point of the scheme spectrum), the coheight of $p$ in $\Spec R$ equals
  the height of $p$'s underlying prime ideal in $\Spec R$ (equivalently:
  the supremum of strict prime-ideal chains $p_0 \subsetneq \cdots
  \subsetneq p$).
\end{lemma}
```

Sketch: the specialization preorder on `Spec R` is the dual of the
inclusion preorder on `PrimeSpectrum R` (folklore; cf. Mathlib
`spec_le_iff` in `AffineSpace.lean`). Coheight in the dual equals
height in the original, by `coheight_orderIso` + `height_toDual`.

Source pointer: `analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md` Decision 2 sketch L259-268.

### Block 3 — The bridge: coheight at a scheme point equals Krull dim of stalk

```latex
\begin{theorem}[coheight to Krull dimension bridge]
  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
  \uses{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding, lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
  Let $X$ be a scheme and $z \in X$ any point. Then
  \[
    \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z).
  \]
\end{theorem}
```

Sketch (5-step assembly per analogist recipe Decision 2 L280-296):
1. Pick an affine open `U` of `X` containing `z` (via
   `exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset`).
2. Let `p` be the prime ideal of `Γ(U)` corresponding to `z` (via
   `IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf`).
3. The stalk `O_{X,z}` is the localization of `Γ(U)` at `p` (via
   `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_stalk`).
4. `ringKrullDim` of the localization equals the height of `p`, via
   `IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height` +
   `Ideal.height_eq_primeHeight`.
5. Identify the height of `p` (in `PrimeSpectrum Γ(U)`) with the coheight
   of `z` in `U.carrier` via Lemma 2, then lift back to coheight of `z`
   in `X.carrier` via Lemma 1.

### Block 4 — Instance: coheight = 1 ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` on stalk

```latex
\begin{lemma}[coheight 1 forces stalk Krull-dim $\leq 1$]
  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
  \uses{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
  Let $X$ be a scheme and $z \in X$ with $\operatorname{coheight}_X(z) = 1$.
  Then `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` holds on the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$.
\end{lemma}
```

Sketch: rewrite via the bridge (Block 3); `coheight z = 1` and
`KrullDimLE 1 ↔ ringKrullDim ≤ 1` gives the result. ~10 LOC in Lean.

This is the load-bearing instance for `CodimOneExtension.lean`'s
`hreg_dim` refactor: after Lane M lands, `hreg_dim` halves into a
Krull-dim half (closed by this instance, given `_hz : coheight z = 1`)
and an `IsRegularLocalRing` half (the Stacks 00TT gap, separate
sub-project).

## Out of scope

103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}

109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."

118	\begin{proof}

127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

48	## Key Files & Permissions

50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:

75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).

3	## Mode
4	api-alignment

6	## Slug

12	## Question
13	
14	`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean` carries 3 sorries blocking the
15	critical-path Lemma 3.3 codim-1 extension argument:
16	
17	1. `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` (Milne 3.1) — the codim-≥2 extension to
18	   a proper target.

22	   — the conjunction `IsRegularLocalRing stalk ∧ ringKrullDim stalk = 1`.

25	- **Stacks 00TT** — smooth ⟹ regular stalks.
26	- **Coheight-to-Krull-dim** — `Order.coheight z = 1 ⟹ ringKrullDim
27	  (X.presheaf.stalk z) = 1`.
28	- **Codim-1 valuative criterion** — rational map indeterminacy is codim ≥ 2.
29	
30	## Project artifact(s)
31	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`:222-271 — private
32	  helper `smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot` (internal
33	  `hreg_dim` sorry).
34	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`:356-368 —
35	  `extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth` (bare sorry).
36	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`:397-411 —
37	  `indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme` (bare sorry).
38	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`:148-151 — the
39	  `[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]` instance argument on
40	  `Scheme.RationalMap.order`, gated on the same coheight bridge.
41	
42	## Decisions identified
43	
44	### Decision 1: Stacks 00TT — does Mathlib have `Algebra.Smooth k A → IsRegularLocalRing (A_p)`?
45	
46	- **Mathlib idiom (NEGATIVE)**: Mathlib's `RegularLocalRing` lives in
47	  `Mathlib.RingTheory.RegularLocalRing.Defs` (79 LOC, a single file).
48	  Cite: `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/RingTheory/RegularLocalRing/Defs.lean:1–79`.
49	  The class `IsRegularLocalRing` extends `IsLocalRing + IsNoetherianRing +
50	  (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R`.
51	
52	  Direct LSP verification:
53	  - `lean_loogle "IsRegularLocalRing"` returns **14 hits, all confined to
54	    `RegularLocalRing/Defs.lean`** — there is no `Algebra.Smooth →
55	    IsRegularLocalRing` or `Algebra.Smooth → IsRegularRing` declaration.
56	  - `grep -rn "IsRegularLocalRing\|IsRegularRing"
57	    .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry` returns **0 hits** —
58	    no scheme-level smoothness-to-regularity bridge.
59	  - `Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.Locus` characterizes `smoothLocus R A` as
60	    `(Module.support A (H1Cotangent R A))ᶜ ∩ Module.freeLocus A Ω[A⁄R]`
61	    (Cite: `Locus.lean:57`) — this captures the H¹-vanishing + free
62	    Kähler-differentials side of regularity but does NOT export an
63	    `IsRegularLocalRing` instance.
64	
65	- **Project's current path**: typed `sorry` packaged as the conjunction
66	  `hreg_dim : IsRegularLocalRing stalk ∧ ringKrullDim stalk = 1`
67	  (`CodimOneExtension.lean:242`).
68	- **Gap**: divergent-and-unavoidable — Mathlib does not provide the
69	  implication at the pinned commit `b80f227`. This is a load-bearing
70	  Stacks tag (00TT) that has not been formalized.
71	- **Cost of divergence**: substantial. The classical Stacks 00TT proof
72	  needs:
73	  - `Algebra.Smooth k A`, `k` field ⟹ `Ω_{A/k}` finitely generated
74	    projective (Mathlib has this via `Algebra.Smooth.flat` +
75	    `smoothLocus_eq_compl_support_inter`).
76	  - At a closed point `p ∈ Spec A` with residue field `κ = κ(p)`, the rank
77	    of `Ω_{A/k} ⊗ κ` equals the relative dimension and equals
78	    `finrank κ ((maximalIdeal A_p)/(maximalIdeal A_p)²)` (the cotangent
79	    space).
80	  - For smooth `A/k` of pure dimension `d`, `ringKrullDim A_p = d` and
81	    `finrank cotangent = d`, hence by Mathlib's
82	    `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` (Cite:
83	    `RegularLocalRing/Defs.lean:65`), `IsRegularLocalRing A_p`.
84	
85	  Estimated LOC for a project-side build of Stacks 00TT at the algebra
86	  level: **~200–300 LOC**, spanning `Module.finrank Ω ⊗ κ = relativeDim`
87	  + `ringKrullDim_eq_relativeDim` (Stacks 00OS) + cotangent-space
88	  identification + the `iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` discharge.
89	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL**. Genuine upstream gap; not
90	  approachable via a small project helper.
91	
92	### Decision 2: Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge — `Order.coheight z = ringKrullDim (X.presheaf.stalk z)`
93	
94	- **Mathlib idiom — pieces exist, the *assembly* is project-side**.
95	  Concretely:
96	
97	  - **Convention reconciliation**: `specializationPreorder` (Mathlib's
98	    spec preorder on a topological space, cite:
99	    `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Topology/Defs/Filter.lean:228`)
100	    uses `x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, i.e. *the generic point is maximal*. On
101	    `Spec R`, this matches the *dual* of the `PrimeSpectrum R` natural
102	    inclusion order: `spec_le_iff` (Cite:
103	    `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/AffineSpace.lean:438-447`)
104	    establishes `p ≤ q (Spec R) ↔ q.asIdeal ≤ p.asIdeal (PrimeSpectrum)`
105	    and gives the example `Preorder (Spec R) = Preorder (PrimeSpectrum R)ᵒᵈ`.
106	
107	  - **`Order.coheight` transports through `OrderIso`**:
108	    `coheight_orderIso : ∀ (f : α ≃o β) (x : α), coheight (f x) = coheight x`
109	    (Cite:
110	    `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Order/KrullDimension.lean:335-336`).
111	
112	  - **`Order.height (PrimeSpectrum) = ringKrullDim (Localization.AtPrime)`**:
113	    `IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height` (Cite:
114	    `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/RingTheory/Ideal/Height.lean:341-348`):
115	    ```
116	    theorem IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim_eq_height (I : Ideal R)
117	        [I.IsPrime] (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [Algebra R A]
118	        [IsLocalization.AtPrime A I] : ringKrullDim A = I.height
119	    ```
120	    Combined with `Ideal.height_eq_primeHeight` (Cite:
121	    `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45`) and
122	    `Ideal.primeHeight = Order.height (in PrimeSpectrum)` (Cite:
123	    `Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:37-38`), we get
124	    `ringKrullDim (Localization.AtPrime p) = Order.height ⟨p, _⟩`
125	    (in `PrimeSpectrum R`).
126	
127	  - **Affine-stalk localization**: `IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_stalk`
128	    (Cite:
129	    `.lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/AffineScheme.lean:806-813`):
130	    ```
131	    theorem isLocalization_stalk (x : U) :
132	        IsLocalization.AtPrime (X.presheaf.stalk x) (hU.primeIdealOf x).asIdeal
133	    ```
134	
135	  - **Existence of affine open**: `exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset`
136	    (Cite: `AffineScheme.lean:271`).
137	
138	  - **Generizations stay in open subsets**: Generic property — if `U`
139	    open and `x ∈ U`, every generization `y ⤳ x` is in `U` (because
140	    opens are stable under generization: `y` is in every open containing
141	    `x`). This is `IsOpen.specializes_iff` style. Mathlib has
142	    `Specializes.mem_open` (Cite: `Topology.Inseparable:96`):
143	    `h : x ⤳ y → IsOpen s → y ∈ s → x ∈ s` — i.e. if `x ⤳ y` (so
144	    `y ∈ closure({x})`) and `y ∈ s` open, then `x ∈ s`.
145	    In spec preorder terms: `y ≤ x` (= `x ⤳ y`) and `y ∈ U` open ⟹
146	    `x ∈ U`. So every strict generization of a point in an open belongs
147	    to that open. ✓ (This lets `Order.coheight z` in `X.carrier` reduce
148	    to `Order.coheight z` in `U` with the subspace topology.)

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ throughout. By a \emph{variety} over $\bar k$
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over $\bar k$
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over $\bar k$.
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to Y$ defined on a dense open subset
54	$U \subseteq X$, where two pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$, $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ are equivalent
55	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$ (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map $f$
56	is \emph{defined at} a point $x \in X$ if there is some representative $(U, \varphi_U)$
57	with $x \in U$; the set of such $x$ is an open subset $\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X$,
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of $f$.
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of $X$?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on $X$ or $Y$ the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if $Y$ is complete (proper over $\bar k$), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-$1$ point of a normal source $X$ (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if $Y$ is a group variety, the difference map
75	    $\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}$ on $X \times X$ has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For $Y$ an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-$1$ extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-$1$ extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	\leanok
187	  [Smooth $\Rightarrow$ DVR at every codim-$1$ point]
188	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
190	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
191	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
192	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
193	  % PDF page 147).
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme $X$ is \emph{regular in
195	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
196	  % local ring $\mathcal O_x$ of $X$ of dimension one is regular."
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
198	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
199	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
200	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
201	  % closed points."
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its generic
203	  % point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at $\eta$ is a discrete
204	  % valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the function field of $X$."
205	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
206	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
207	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$ and let $\eta \in X$ be a point with
208	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a
209	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field $K(X)$ of $X$.
210	\end{lemma}
211	
212	\begin{proof}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  By smoothness, $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is regular for every closed point $x \in X$
216	  (Hartshorne I.5.1). Localising a regular local ring at a prime ideal yields a regular
217	  local ring; in particular, for $\eta \in X$ with
218	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is regular
219	  of Krull dimension $1$. A regular local ring of Krull dimension $1$ is exactly a
220	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
221	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field $K(X)$ of $X$ by
222	  irreducibility of $X$ (the unique generic point of $X$ lies above $\eta$, and the
223	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).

227	  instance/lemma stating that $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a Mathlib
228	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
229	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
230	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta$ = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
231	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance

239	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-$\geq 2$ extension}
240	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
241	
242	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
243	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-$1$
244	point.
245	
246	\begin{theorem}
247	
248	\leanok
249	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-$\geq 2$ extension to a complete target]
250	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
252	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim_one_indeterminacy, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
253	        cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
254	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
255	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
256	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
257	  % \dashrightarrow W$ from a normal variety $V$ to a complete variety $W$ is defined
258	  % on an open subset $U$ of $V$ whose complement $V \smallsetminus U$ has codimension
259	  % $\geq 2$.}"
260	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
261	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $Y$ be a complete variety over
262	  $\bar k$, and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map. Then the
263	  indeterminacy locus $Z(f)$ (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
264	  $\geq 2$ in $X$; equivalently, $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
265	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
266	
267	  Specialising further: if in addition $f$ \emph{is} codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
268	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of $X$ is contained in $Z(f)$), then
269	  $\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X$ and $f$ extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
270	  \to Y$. The extension is unique because $X$ is reduced and $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
271	  in $X$.
272	\end{theorem}
273	
274	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that $V$ is a curve. Thus we are
275	% given a nonsingular curve $V$ and a regular map $\varphi \colon U \to W$ from an
276	% open subset of $V$ which we want to extend to $V$. Consider the maps
277	% [diagram: $U \hookrightarrow V$, $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
278	% \subseteq V \times W$, $U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
279	% $V \times W$ to $V$ and to $W$]
280	% Let $U'$ be the image of $U$ in $V \times W$, and let $Z$ be its closure. The image
281	% of $Z$ in $V$ is closed (because $W$ is complete), and contains $U$ (the composite
282	% $U \to V$ is the given inclusion), and so $Z$ maps onto $V$. The maps $U \to U'
283	% \to U$ are isomorphisms. Therefore, $Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
284	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
285	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
286	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map $V \times W \to W$
287	% to $Z\ (\simeq V)$ is the extension of $\varphi$ to $V$ we are seeking.
288	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
289	% $U$ be the largest subset on which $\varphi$ is defined, and suppose that $V
290	% \smallsetminus U$ has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor $Z$ in $V
291	% \smallsetminus U$. Because $V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
292	% valuation ring $\mathcal O_Z$ with field of fractions $k(V)$. The map $\varphi$
293	% defines a morphism of schemes $\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W$, which the
294	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
295	% morphism $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W$. This implies that $\varphi$
296	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets $Z$ in a nonempty set,
297	% which is a contradiction."
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim_one_indeterminacy, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
300	        cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
301	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
302	
303	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-$1$ components.} Suppose for contradiction that $Z(f)$
304	  contains a prime divisor $W \subseteq X$ (i.e.\ a codim-$1$ irreducible component).
305	  Let $\eta \in W$ be its generic point, so $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1$. By
306	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
307	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field $K(X)$. Any representative
308	  $(U, \varphi_U) \in f$ has $U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset$ and so determines
309	  a $\bar k$-algebra map from the function field $K(Y)$ of $Y$ to $K(X)$: explicitly,
310	  $\varphi_U$ defines a morphism of schemes $\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y$ (since
311	  $\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))$ is the generic point of $X$). By the valuative criterion
312	  of properness applied to the complete variety $Y$
313	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
314	  extends to a morphism $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y$. Standard
315	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood $V$ of $\eta$ in
316	  $X$ on which $f$ has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
317	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)$, contradicting $\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)$. Hence no codim-$1$
318	  component of $X$ lies in $Z(f)$, i.e.\ $Z(f)$ has codimension $\geq 2$.
319	
320	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-$\geq 2$ points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
321	  $\Rightarrow$ extension" half).} Suppose now that $Z(f)$ has codimension $\geq 2$
322	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce $Z(f) = \emptyset$ under the additional input
323	  that ``$f$ is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
324	  point $x \in Z(f)$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2$. By
325	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
326	  $\mathcal O_{X, x}$, we have
327	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2$. The sheaf
328	  $\mathcal O_X$ on the punctured neighbourhood $V \smallsetminus \{x\}$
329	  (any small affine neighbourhood $V \ni x$) has $H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$
330	  and $H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0$ (the depth-$\geq 2$ characterisation of
331	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
332	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of $Y$ around the
333	  closure $\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}$ along $f$; the resulting sections of
334	  $\mathcal O_X$ on $V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)$ extend uniquely to sections on $V$,
335	  yielding a regular representative of $f$ on $V$. Hence $x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)$,
336	  contradicting $x \in Z(f)$. Therefore $Z(f) = \emptyset$ and $f$ extends to a
337	  regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X \to Y$.
338	
339	  Uniqueness of $\widetilde f$ is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
340	  principle: two morphisms $X \to Y$ that agree on the dense open $\mathrm{Dom}(f)$
341	  agree on all of $X$.
342	\end{proof}
343	
344	\begin{remark}
345	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
346	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
347	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
348	  Step~2 uses the depth-$\geq 2$ extension property at a codim-$\geq 2$ regular
349	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
350	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension $d$
351	  $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay of depth $d$). For our application the source $X$ is a
352	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
353	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
354	
355	  Concretely, when $X$ is a smooth surface (e.g.\ $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ or
356	  $C \times C$ for a smooth projective curve $C$), the codim-$\geq 2$ closed points of
357	  $X$ are exactly the closed points of $X$ (because $\dim X = 2$), and at each such
358	  point $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is regular of dimension $2$, hence Cohen--Macaulay of
359	  depth $2$. The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
360	  symmetric-product rational map $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ along the Abel--Jacobi
361	  birational equivalence to $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
362	\end{remark}

449	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
450	  \]
451	  where $\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G$ is the group-variety inverse and
452	  $m \colon G \times G \to G$ is the group law. The map $\Phi$ is rational by
453	  composition: its domain is at least $U \times U \subseteq X \times X$, which is dense
454	  because $U$ is dense in $X$.
455	
456	  \emph{Sub-step 2: $\Phi$-defined at $(x, x)$ $\Leftrightarrow$ $\varphi$-defined at
457	  $x$.} If $\varphi$ is defined at $x$, the explicit formula $\Phi(x, x) = e$ (the
458	  identity of $G$) shows $\Phi$ is defined at $(x, x)$. Conversely, if $\Phi$ is
459	  defined at $(x, x)$, openness of $\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$ in $X \times X$ produces an
460	  open set $W \ni (x, x)$ on which $\Phi$ is defined; by projection (taking the slice
461	  $\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W$ for some open $W' \subseteq X$, possibly shrinking
462	  $W'$ so $\varphi$ is defined on $W'$ as well --- $W'$ need not contain $x$), the
463	  identity
464	  \[
465	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
466	    \qquad u \in W',
467	  \]
468	  exhibits a regular representative of $\varphi$ at $x$. Hence $x \in
469	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)$ if and only if $(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
470	
471	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map $\Phi$
472	  pulls $\mathcal O_{G, e}$ back to a $\bar k$-subalgebra of the function field
473	  $K(X \times X)$:
474	  \[
475	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
476	  \]
477	  Because $\Phi$ sends $(x, x)$ to $e$ wherever it is defined, $\Phi$ is defined at
478	  $(x, x)$ if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
479	  \times X, (x, x)}$ (i.e.\ every local function on $G$ near $e$ has no pole at
480	  $(x, x)$ after pullback).
481	
482	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension $1$.} The variety $X \times X$ is
483	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over $\bar k$), so the Weil-divisor
484	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
485	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on $X \times X$. For any nonzero rational function
486	  $f \in K(X \times X)^\ast$ decompose its divisor as
487	  \[
488	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
489	  \]
490	  with $\mathrm{div}(f)_0$ and $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ effective and
491	  $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0$ (Milne's notation, matching the
492	  project's $\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y$ of
493	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: $\mathrm{div}(f)_0$ collects the prime divisors with
494	  $\ord_Y(f) > 0$ and $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ those with $\ord_Y(f) < 0$). The local
495	  ring at $(x, x)$ can be characterised as
496	  \[
497	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
498	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
499	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
500	  \]
501	  Suppose $f \in K(X \times X)^\ast$ is not in $\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}$;
502	  equivalently, $(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)$. The pole divisor
503	  $\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty$ is a finite sum of prime divisors of $X \times X$ (i.e.\ a
504	  closed subset of pure codimension $1$ in $X \times X$). Its intersection with the
505	  diagonal $\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X$ is a closed subset of pure codimension $1$
506	  in $\Delta_X$ (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
507	  divisor of $X \times X$ either contains $\Delta_X$ or meets it in pure codimension
508	  $1$; on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
509	  applied to the local equation of $\Delta_X$ inside $X \times X$).
510	
511	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose $f \colon X \dashrightarrow G$ is not defined on all of
512	  $X$. Pick $x \in Z(f)$. By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
513	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})$ with $(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
514	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
515	  \Delta_X$ is pure codimension $1$ in $\Delta_X$, passing through $(x, x)$.
516	  Identifying $\Delta_X$ with $X$ via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
517	  closed subset of $X$ has pure codimension $1$ and passes through $x$. By Sub-step~2,
518	  every point of this codim-$1$ subset is a point where $\Phi$ is undefined on the
519	  diagonal, hence where $\varphi$ is undefined. Thus $Z(\varphi)$ contains a pure
520	  codim-$1$ closed subset passing through $x$; since $x \in Z(\varphi)$ was arbitrary,
521	  every irreducible component of $Z(\varphi)$ has codimension exactly $1$, i.e.\
522	  $Z(\varphi)$ is of pure codimension $1$.
523	\end{proof}
524	
525	\begin{remark}
526	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
527	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on $G$
528	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of $G$ as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
529	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety $X \times X$ (Hartshorne~II.6, or
530	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
531	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
532	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on $X \times X$. None of these has a characteristic
533	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
534	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
535	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
536	\end{remark}

538	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
539	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
540	
541	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-$1$ ruling-out) goes
542	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
543	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
544	the extension fails at a prime divisor $W$ if and only if some affine coordinate of
545	$f$ around the image $\overline{f(\eta_W)}$ has a strictly negative order
546	$\ord_W(\cdot) < 0$. This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
547	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
548	
549	\begin{theorem}
550	
551	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
552	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
553	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:indeterminacy_locus,
554	        lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
555	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
556	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
557	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
558	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
559	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
560	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
561	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
562	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
563	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
564	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
565	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
566	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
567	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
568	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
569	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
570	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
571	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
572	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation $v_Y$ associated to a
573	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}$ as the
574	  % subring of $K(X)$ on which $v_Y \geq 0$) (read from
575	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
576	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its generic
577	  % point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at $\eta$ is a discrete
578	  % valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the function field of $X$. We call the
579	  % corresponding discrete valuation $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
580	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function on $X$.
581	  % Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has a \emph{zero} along
582	  % $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$ has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of
583	  % order $-v_Y(f)$."
584	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ and the order map).}
585	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over $\bar k$, let $Y$ be a variety over $\bar k$,
586	  let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map, and let $W \subseteq X$ be a
587	  prime divisor with generic point $\eta_W \in W$ (so
588	  $\codim_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1$). Let $(U, \varphi_U)$ be any representative of
589	  $f$ with $U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset$. The following are equivalent:
590	  \begin{enumerate}
591	    \item $f$ is defined at $\eta_W$ (i.e.\ $\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)$, equivalently
592	      $W \not\subseteq Z(f)$).
593	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood $V \subseteq Y$ of
594	      the generic image of $f$ near $W$, every regular function $g \in
595	      \mathcal O_Y(V)$ pulls back to a rational function $\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
596	      satisfying
597	      \[
598	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
599	      \]
600	  \end{enumerate}
601	
602	  Consequently: $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free
603	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
604	  $W \subseteq X$ and every affine open $V \subseteq Y$ as above, the pullback
605	  $\varphi_U^\ast(g)$ has $\ord_W \geq 0$ for all $g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)$.
606	\end{theorem}
607	
608	\begin{proof}
609	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:codim1_cycles, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr,
610	        thm:codim_one_extension}
611	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}$ is a
612	  DVR with fraction field $K(X)$ and uniformiser determined by $W$; by
613	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly $\ord_W$. The local ring at
614	  $\eta_W$ is therefore characterised inside $K(X)$ as the subring
615	  \[
616	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
617	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
618	  \]
619	  The rational map $f$ is defined at $\eta_W$ if and only if, for any affine open
620	  $V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y$ around the image $\overline{f(\eta_W)}$,
621	  the pullback $\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)$ lands in
622	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}$: this is the standard local characterisation of where a
623	  rational map is regular at a codim-$1$ point of a smooth variety (it is the
624	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
625	  equivalent to $\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0$ for every $g \in A$, which is
626	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~$\Leftrightarrow$~(2) is therefore established.
627	
628	  For the consequence, $f$ is codim-$1$-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor $W$ is
629	  contained in $Z(f)$, which by the equivalence above is iff the $\ord_W \geq 0$
630	  condition holds for every prime divisor $W$.
631	\end{proof}
632	
633	\begin{remark}
634	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
635	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
636	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
637	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
638	  isolation, the abstract codim-$\geq 2$ statement
639	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
640	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
641	  $\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}$ API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
642	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-$0$ classifying-map
643	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
644	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
645	\end{remark}

728	\section{Out of scope}
729	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
730	
731	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
732	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
733	scope:
734	\begin{itemize}
735	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular $\Rightarrow$
736	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
737	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
738	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
739	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
740	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
741	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
742	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
743	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
744	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
745	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
746	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
747	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
748	    $\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A$ / unirational$\dashrightarrow A$ constancy
749	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
750	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-$0$ base case is
751	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut
752	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
753	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
754	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on $X \times X$ for $X$ a curve.} The proof of
755	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
756	    smooth surface $X \times X$ but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
757	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-$1$
758	    decomposition $\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty$ on a nonsingular surface is
759	    used. The bilinear degree formula on $X \times X$ for $X$ a curve (intersection
760	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
761	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
762	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
763	    introduced.
764	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
765	    project's positive-genus arm runs over $\bar k$ throughout; descent of the
766	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
767	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
768	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
769	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
770	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
771	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for $G$ a group variety is sharper than any
772	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original $X$
773	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
774	\end{itemize}

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface $S$ over a field --- such as $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$
28	or the product $C \times C$ of a smooth projective curve $C$ with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension $2$. To extend a rational map
30	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from $S$ to an abelian variety across a codimension-$2$ closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} $2$, hence a rational function with no codimension-$1$ poles on a neighbourhood of $x$
33	extends across $x$. The depth $\geq 2$ inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring $R$ one has $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$,
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension $d$ admits an $R$-regular sequence of length $d$ generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$, i.e.\ $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its $\text{Ext}$ reformulation
47	    (Definition~\ref{def:depth} and Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~\ref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~\ref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.

94	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0$ and for each $i$, the element $f_i$ is a non-zero-divisor on
95	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M$. The empty sequence is regular on $M$ iff $M \neq 0$ (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention $\text{depth}(0) = \infty$
97	above is the usual one).

100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the $\text{Ext}$ characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).

162	\label{sec:ab_pd}

164	\begin{definition}
165	
166	\leanok
167	  [Projective dimension]
168	  \label{def:projective_dimension}

189	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each $P_i$
190	  free of finite rank and each transition map $P_{i+1} \to P_i$ landing in $\mathfrak{m} P_i$.

\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight $=$ Krull-dim-of-stalk
% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
%      $=$ localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:coheight_setup}

A point $z$ of a scheme $X$ has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at $z$}, written
$\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$, is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
of chains of prime ideals in the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$. The
\emph{coheight} of $z$ in $X$, written $\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)$, is a
topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
topological space of $X$: the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
chains $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in $X$, where $\prec$ is the
strict specialisation order $x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x$.

These two invariants agree:
\[
  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
\]
which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
statement that the dimension of the local ring at $z$ counts irreducible
closed subsets of $X$ containing $z$ --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.

\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
codim-$1$ use site:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
    \[
      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
    \]
    inside the private helper
    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the

\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
\label{sec:coheight_topology}

% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
% formulation.

\begin{lemma}
  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
  Let $X$ be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
  let $U \subseteq X$ be an open subset. For any $z \in U$,
  \[
    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
  \]
  where on the right $U$ carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
  z \in U\rangle$ denotes $z$ viewed as a point of $U$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers $n$, of the
  set of $n$ for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
  $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in the relevant
  specialisation preorder (with $\prec$ denoting strict specialisation). It
  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
  strict-generisation chains starting at $z$ in $X$ and those starting at the
  corresponding point of $U$.

  \emph{Chains in $U$ embed into $X$.} The inclusion $U \hookrightarrow X$ is
  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if $y \rightsquigarrow x$ in $U$
  then $y \rightsquigarrow x$ in $X$, and distinct points of $U$ remain
  distinct in $X$. Hence every strict-generisation chain in $U$ starting at
  $z$ lifts to one in $X$ of the same length.

  \emph{Chains in $X$ starting at $z$ all lie in $U$.} Conversely, suppose
  $z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ is a strict-generisation chain
  in $X$ with $a_0 = z \in U$. For each $i \geq 0$, the relation $a_i \succeq
  z$ (in the specialisation preorder) is $z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
  $a_i$ is in the closure of $\{z\}$ \emph{viewed from above}; in our
  convention the strict order $a \prec b$ records $b \rightsquigarrow a$
  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so $a_i \succeq z$ in $X$ means
  $a_i \rightsquigarrow z$ in $X$. By the Mathlib lemma
  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
  $a \rightsquigarrow z$ with $z$ in an open set $U$ forces $a \in U$, since
  $U$ is open and contains $z$, hence contains every point whose closure
  contains $z$ --- equivalently every $a$ with $a \rightsquigarrow z$),
  every $a_i$ lies in $U$. Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle$ in $U$ of the same length.

  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
  chains starting at $z$ (resp.\ at $\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$) and
  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
\end{proof}

\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}

% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.

\begin{lemma}
  [Coheight on $\Spec R$ equals height in $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R$]
  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
  Let $R$ be a commutative ring and let $p \in \Spec R$ (viewed as a point of
  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
  \[
    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
  \]
  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
  prime-ideal chains $\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}$
  ending at the prime $\mathfrak{p}$ corresponding to $p$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  The specialisation preorder on $\Spec R$ is the dual of the natural
  inclusion preorder on $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$. Concretely, for
  $p, q \in \Spec R$ with underlying primes $\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}$,
  the relation $p \leq q$ in the specialisation preorder (which is
  $q \rightsquigarrow p$ in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
  containment $\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}$. This is the content of
  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on $\Spec R$
  equals the order-dual of the preorder on $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R$).

  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of $p$ in $\Spec R$
  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at $p$,
  i.e.\ ascending chains $p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n$ in the
  specialisation preorder) translates to height of $\mathfrak{p}$ in
  $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$ (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
  identification $\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}$
  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
\end{proof}

\section{The coheight $=$ Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
\label{sec:coheight_bridge}

% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}

\begin{theorem}
  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
  \uses{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding, lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
  Let $X$ be a scheme and let $z \in X$ be any point. Then
  \[
    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
  \]
  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at $z$ equals the coheight of $z$ in
  the underlying topological space of $X$ (with respect to the specialisation
  preorder).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding, lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
  arguing from a single textbook reference.

  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of $z$.} By
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
  applied to the open set $X$ itself, there exists an affine open
  $U \subseteq X$ with $z \in U$. Write $\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U$
  for $z$ viewed as a point of the subscheme $U$.

  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to $z$ inside $U$.} Since
  $U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ via the affine equivalence
  $U \simeq \Spec R$ (with $R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$), the point
  $\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$ corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,$\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle$.

  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of $\Gamma(U)$ at
  $\mathfrak{p}$.} The classical affine-stalk identification says
  $\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}$. In Mathlib this is the
  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
  $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ as a localisation of $R$ at $\mathfrak{p}$ in the
  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.

  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
  $\mathfrak{p}$.} The Mathlib lemma
  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
  \[
    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
  \]
  where $\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})$ is the prime-ideal height in
  $\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)$. By
  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
  $\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}$ used in
  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.

  \emph{Step 5: lift back from $U$ to $X$.} By
  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to $R$ and the
  affine identification $U \simeq \Spec R$,
  \[
    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
  \]
  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
  $U \subseteq X$ promotes the right-hand side back to
  $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)$. Concatenating the equalities from
  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
\end{proof}

\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1$}{KrullDimLE 1}}
\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}

% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-$1$ case used in
% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.

\begin{lemma}
  [Coheight $= 1$ forces stalk $\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1$]
  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
  \uses{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
  Let $X$ be a scheme and let $z \in X$ be a point with
  $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$. Then the stalk $\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$
  has Krull dimension at most $1$, equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
  $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$.
  The Mathlib predicate $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R$ unfolds to
  $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1$, so the inequality $1 \leq 1$
  discharges the goal.
\end{proof}

\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
\label{sec:coheight_consumers}

\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
The private helper
\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
\texttt{sorry} of the form
\[
  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
\]
After this chapter's
\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
two independent goals:
\begin{itemize}
  \item The right conjunct $\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1$
    is discharged by
    $\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1$ (the standing codim-$1$ hypothesis
    on $z$) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
    pair $\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1$ (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
    and $\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1$ (also from the bridge, since
    $1 \leq 1$) gives equality.
  \item The left conjunct $\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}$
    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A $\to$ IsRegularLocalRing $A_{\mathfrak{p}}$} at
    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is $\sim 200$--$300$~LOC of
    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
    chapter).
\end{itemize}
Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
one remains as a named Mathlib gap.

\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
argument
\[
  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
\]
at every use site. Because $Y$ is a prime divisor of $X$ (so
$\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1$ by the definition of a prime
divisor as a codim-$1$ irreducible closed subset),
\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
automatically from the codim-$1$ hypothesis on $Y$, so the explicit instance
argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.

\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-$1$ infrastructure.} Any subsequent
project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-$1$ point has
Krull dim $\leq 1$'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-$1$
argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
The instance form
\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.

\section{Lean encoding}
\label{sec:coheight_lean_encoding}
